content:
# Acts Chapter 19 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 248-250 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_019_page_001.jpg, ACT_019_page_002.jpg, ACT_019_page_003.jpg

---

**1** While Apollos was in Corinth, Paul traveled through the highland regions and came down to Ephesus, where he found a group of disciples. **2** He asked them, "Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you came to faith?" They told him, "We have not even heard that there is a Holy Spirit." **3** So he asked, "Then what baptism did you receive?" They answered, "The baptism of John." **4** Paul explained, "John's baptism was a baptism of repentance. He told the people to put their trust in the one coming after him — that is, in Jesus." **5** When they heard this, they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus. **6** And when Paul placed his hands on them, the Holy Spirit came on them, and they began speaking in tongues and prophesying. **7** There were about twelve men in all.

**8** Paul went into the synagogue and spoke boldly for three months, engaging people in discussion and persuading them about the kingdom of God. **9** But when some of them became stubborn and refused to believe, publicly slandering the Way in front of the whole congregation, Paul broke away from them. He took the disciples with him and held daily discussions in the lecture hall of Tyrannus. **10** This went on for two years, so that everyone living in the province of Asia — both Jews and Greeks — heard the word of the Lord.

**11** God was doing extraordinary miracles through Paul's hands — **12** to the point that even handkerchiefs and work-aprons that had touched his skin were carried to the sick, and their diseases left them and evil spirits came out.

**13** Some Jewish exorcists who traveled from place to place tried invoking the name of the Lord Jesus over people who had evil spirits. They would say, "I command you by the Jesus that Paul preaches!" **14** Seven sons of a Jewish chief priest named Sceva were doing this. **15** But the evil spirit answered them: "Jesus I know, and Paul I am well acquainted with — but who are you?" **16** Then the man with the evil spirit leaped on them, overpowered them all, and beat them so savagely that they fled from that house naked and bleeding.

**17** This became known to everyone living in Ephesus, both Jews and Greeks, and a deep fear fell on all of them. The name of the Lord Jesus was held in the highest honor. **18** Many who had come to believe now came forward, openly confessing and exposing their former practices. **19** A large number of those who had practiced sorcery brought their scrolls together and burned them publicly. When they added up the value, it came to fifty thousand pieces of silver. **20** In this powerful way, the word of the Lord kept spreading and growing in strength.

**21** After all this had happened, Paul resolved in the Spirit to travel through Macedonia and Achaia and then go on to Jerusalem. He said, "After I have been there, I must also see Rome." **22** He sent two of his assistants, Timothy and Erastus, ahead to Macedonia, while he himself stayed on in the province of Asia for a time.

**23** Around that time, a major disturbance erupted over the Way. **24** A silversmith named Demetrius, who made silver shrines of Artemis and brought in considerable business for the craftsmen, **25** gathered them together along with workers in related trades and said: "Men, you know very well that our prosperity comes from this trade. **26** And you can see and hear for yourselves that this Paul has persuaded and turned away large numbers of people — not only in Ephesus but in nearly all of the province of Asia — by telling them that gods made by human hands are not gods at all. **27** There is a danger not only that our trade will fall into disrepute, but that the temple of the great goddess Artemis will be regarded as worthless, and she herself — whom all of Asia and the entire world worship — will be stripped of her majesty."

**28** When they heard this, they were filled with rage and began shouting, "Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!" **29** The whole city was thrown into chaos. The crowd seized Gaius and Aristarchus, Paul's Macedonian traveling companions, and rushed together into the theater. **30** Paul wanted to go before the crowd, but the disciples would not let him. **31** Even some of the provincial officials who were friendly to him sent an urgent message begging him not to set foot in the theater.

**32** Meanwhile, the assembly was in total confusion. Some were shouting one thing, some another. Most of the people had no idea why they had even gathered. **33** Some in the crowd put forward Alexander, and the Jews pushed him to the front. Alexander motioned with his hand, trying to make a defense speech to the people. **34** But the moment they recognized he was a Jew, a single shout went up from them all, and for about two hours they kept chanting: "Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!"

**35** Finally the city clerk quieted the crowd and said: "People of Ephesus! Who in all the world does not know that the city of the Ephesians is the guardian of the temple of the great Artemis and of the sacred stone that fell from heaven? **36** Since these facts are beyond dispute, you need to calm down and not do anything reckless. **37** You have brought these men here, but they have neither robbed temples nor blasphemed our goddess. **38** So if Demetrius and the craftsmen with him have a legal complaint against anyone, the courts are in session and there are proconsuls — let them bring charges against each other there. **39** But if you are looking for anything beyond that, it must be settled in a lawful assembly. **40** As it stands, we are in danger of being charged with rioting over today's events, since there is no legitimate reason we can give to justify this mob." **41** And with these words, he dismissed the assembly.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

This chapter is a masterwork of narrative escalation — from a quiet doctrinal correction among twelve men to a citywide riot involving thousands. The translation aims to capture that dramatic arc.

**Verses 1-7 — The Incomplete Disciples:** Sinaiticus reads the question in v.2 with "ei pneuma hagion elabete pisteusantes" — the aorist participle "pisteusantes" (having believed) is rendered dynamically as "when you came to faith" to capture the temporal sense. These are sincere believers with incomplete knowledge. The phrase "we have not even heard that there is a Holy Spirit" reflects genuine ignorance of the Spirit's coming at Pentecost, not ignorance of the Spirit's existence in Hebrew scripture.

**Verse 9 — The Hall of Tyrannus:** "Schole Tyrannou" — Tyrannus was likely a philosopher or rhetorician who owned or operated a lecture hall. Paul essentially rented classroom space for his movement. The Western text adds "from the fifth to the tenth hour" (11 AM to 4 PM), the hot midday hours when the hall would have been empty, but Sinaiticus omits this detail.

**Verses 13-16 — The Sons of Sceva:** The Sinaiticus text presents this with vivid economy. The evil spirit's reply in v.15 is devastatingly dismissive — "Jesus I know (ginosko), and Paul I am well acquainted with (epistamai)" uses two different Greek verbs for "know," suggesting different levels of recognition. The spirit recognizes genuine spiritual authority and contemptuously rejects counterfeits. "Overpowered them all" (amphoterov/kata-kurieusan auton) — Sinaiticus uses "amphoterov" which strictly means "both," but the context requires "all" since seven sons are named. This is a known Sinaiticus reading.

**Verse 19 — Fifty Thousand Silver Pieces:** The book-burning scene is one of the most dramatic economic details in Acts. Fifty thousand silver drachmas was an enormous sum — roughly 150 years of wages for a common laborer. These were not cheap trinkets but expensive magical papyri, the kind Ephesus was famous for ("Ephesia grammata"). The public burning was a deliberate, costly break with the past.

**Verses 23-41 — The Riot:** Luke's account of the Demetrius riot is among the finest pieces of narrative writing in the New Testament. The translation preserves the chaos — the confused crowd, the two-hour chanting, the failed attempt by Alexander, and the masterful speech of the city clerk (grammateus) who defuses the situation with bureaucratic logic. His argument is purely civic, not theological: you risk Roman intervention. The phrase "the sacred stone that fell from heaven" (v.35) refers to a meteorite or ancient cult image believed to be of divine origin — a detail confirming the historical specificity of the account.

**Verse 40 — "Charged with rioting":** The Greek "staseos" (sedition/riot) carried severe legal consequences under Roman law. The city clerk's warning is not hypothetical — Rome had suppressed civic assemblies in other cities for less. This was a real threat to Ephesian civic autonomy.

-- PNEUMA
